---
title: "Glossary"
subtitle: "Plain-English definitions for every term named in this curriculum"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.preflight-check}
This glossary is the canonical, plain-English reference for every term any
lesson formally introduces in its `## Name` beat. Each entry gives:

- **Plain-English one-liner.** What the term means, in words you could say
  aloud to a friend.
- **First named in.** The lesson where this term is first defined — your
  starting point if you want the full story, the worked examples, and the
  "where this lives in the world" connections.

This page grows with the curriculum. If a term is missing from a recently
written lesson, that's a bug — please open an issue.
:::

## How to use this page

You can land on this page two ways:

1. **Navbar → Glossary.** Browse alphabetically, or use the table of contents
   on the right.
2. **Inline link from a lesson.** Many lessons cross-link to specific entries.
   Clicking returns you to the same scroll position via your browser's back
   button.

The glossary is *not* a substitute for the lessons. A definition tells you
what a thing is; a lesson teaches you why it matters. Use the **First named
in** link whenever you want the full picture.

---

## A

### Addition {#addition}

Combining two numbers into a total — pictured on the number line as a hop to
the right by the second number's size.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Algebraic connectivity {#algebraic-connectivity}

The second-smallest eigenvalue $\lambda_2(L)$ of the graph Laplacian — small
for graphs with bottlenecks, large for robustly connected ones (Fiedler
1973).

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/14-spectral-graph-theory/index.qmd)

### Algebraic expression {#algebraic-expression}

A recipe built from numbers, variables, and operations, like $2x + 5$ or
$(a + b)/2$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### Analytic function {#analytic-function}

A complex function $f: D \subseteq \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ whose derivative
$f'(z)$ exists as a complex limit independent of the direction of approach.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Angle {#angle}

A measure of the turn between two lines that meet at a common point. Measured
in degrees: a full turn is $360°$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Antiderivative {#antiderivative}

A function whose derivative gives back the original function. If $F'(x) = f(x)$,
then $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ — and so is $F + C$ for any constant.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 9 — The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/09-fundamental-theorem/index.qmd)

### Area {#area}

The size of the surface inside a flat shape, measured in square units
(m², cm²).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

### Arithmetic sequence {#arithmetic-sequence}

A sequence in which each term is the previous term plus a fixed amount,
called the common difference. Example: $3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

## B

### Base {#base}

Two related meanings, both about the number that "sets the scale":

1. **Base of a place-value system.** The number on which a place-value system
   is built. Base 10 (everyday), base 2 (binary, used by computers), base 16
   (hex, used by programmers), base 60 (Babylonian, still used for hours and
   minutes).

2. **Base of an exponent.** In an expression like $a^n$, the number $a$ that
   is being multiplied by itself.

**First named in (place-value sense):** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)
**First named in (exponent sense):** [Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)

### Basis {#basis}

A linearly independent set of vectors whose span is the whole space;
equivalently, a minimal spanning set or a maximal independent set.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Bayes' theorem {#bayes-theorem}

$P(A \mid B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) / P(B)$ — the rule for flipping a
conditional probability. Turns "what you can measure" into "what you actually
want to know."

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)

### Bellman equation {#bellman-equation}

The recursion $V(s) = \mathbb{E}[R(s,a) + \gamma V(s')]$ defining the value
of a state as immediate reward plus discounted future value; for the
optimal value function the expectation is replaced by a max over actions,
and the resulting operator is a $\gamma$-contraction with a unique fixed
point.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 6 — Reinforcement Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)

### Binomial distribution {#binomial-distribution}

The PMF $P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ counting the number of
successes in $n$ independent trials, each with probability $p$. Mean is $np$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Binomial theorem {#binomial-theorem}

The identity $(a+b)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r} b^r$ — every
term of the expansion is a binomial coefficient times a single power product.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 12 — The Binomial Theorem](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/12-binomial-theorem/index.qmd)

### Borrow {#borrow}

The bookkeeping step in column-subtraction: when a column doesn't have enough
to subtract from, you take ten from the column to its left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Burnside's lemma {#burnsides-lemma}

The number of orbits of a group action equals the average number of points
fixed by each group element. Despite the name, due to Cauchy and Frobenius.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/03-group-actions/index.qmd)

## C

### Carry {#carry}

The bookkeeping step in column-addition: when a column's total exceeds 9,
the overflow moves into the column to its left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Cauchy-Riemann equations {#cauchy-riemann-equations}

The conditions $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$ on the real and imaginary parts
$f = u + iv$ that characterise complex differentiability.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Cauchy sequence {#cauchy-sequence}

A sequence whose terms become arbitrarily close to one another: for every
$\epsilon > 0$ there exists $N$ with $|a_m - a_n| < \epsilon$ for all
$m, n \ge N$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Cauchy's theorem {#cauchys-theorem}

For an analytic $f$ in a simply-connected region,
$\oint_C f(z)\, dz = 0$ for every closed contour $C$. The keystone of complex
analysis.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Central Limit Theorem {#central-limit-theorem}

For i.i.d. variables with finite variance, the standardised sample mean
converges in distribution to a standard normal — regardless of the original
distribution.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Chain rule {#chain-rule}

$(f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ — derivative of a composition is "outer
derivative times inner derivative."

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Circle {#circle}

The set of points at a fixed distance (the radius) from a chosen centre.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Class number {#class-number}

The size $h_K = |\mathrm{Cl}(K)|$ of the ideal class group of a number field
$K$ — equivalently, the number of equivalence classes of fractional ideals
modulo principal ideals. $h_K = 1$ exactly when $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID
(and a UFD); larger $h_K$ measures how badly element-level unique
factorisation fails.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 12 — Algebraic Number Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/12-algebraic-number-theory/index.qmd)

### Combination {#combination}

A selection of $r$ items from $n$ where order does not matter:
$\binom{n}{r} = n!/(r!(n-r)!)$. Counts the $r$-element subsets of an $n$-set.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Commutative {#commutative}

A property of an operation: the order of the two inputs doesn't change the
answer. Addition and multiplication are commutative; subtraction and division
are not.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Compactness {#compactness}

A topological tameness property: every open cover has a finite subcover. In
$\mathbb{R}^n$ this is "closed and bounded" (Heine-Borel); in general it is
the property that lets continuous functions attain their max and min and
turns infinite arguments into finite ones.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/02-topology/index.qmd)

### Complex number {#complex-number}

A number $z = a + bi$ with $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ and $i^2 = -1$. Lives as a
point in the complex plane.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Completeness {#completeness}

A space is complete if every Cauchy sequence in it converges to a limit in
the space; $\mathbb{R}$ is complete, $\mathbb{Q}$ is not.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Composite {#composite}

A whole number $\ge 2$ that is not prime — equivalently, has at least three
divisors.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Condition number {#condition-number}

For a matrix, $\kappa(A) = \|A\| \cdot \|A^{-1}\|$. Measures how much input
error a linear system $Ax = b$ amplifies in the output. Large $\kappa$ =
ill-conditioned.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Conditional probability {#conditional-probability}

$P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ — the probability of $A$ once you restrict
the sample space to outcomes where $B$ has already happened.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)

### Confidence interval {#confidence-interval}

A random interval whose construction catches the true parameter with a
stated long-run frequency (e.g. 95%) under repeated sampling.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

### Continuous function {#continuous-function}

A function whose graph can be traced through a point (or interval) without
lifting the pen — formally, $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Convergence {#convergence}

A sequence $a_n$ converges to $L$ if, for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists
$N$ such that $n \ge N$ implies $|a_n - L| < \epsilon$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 14 — Sequences, Series, and Convergence](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd)

### Convex function {#convex-function}

A function whose graph lies below every chord — bowl-shaped, with no internal
bumps; every local minimum is automatically the global minimum.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 9 — Convex Optimisation](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/09-convex-optimization/index.qmd)

### Convolution theorem {#convolution-theorem}

Convolution in the time domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the
frequency domain (and vice versa) — the algorithmic miracle behind FFT-based
filtering.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### Coordinate plane {#coordinate-plane}

Two number lines crossed at right angles, used to give every point in the
plane a unique address $(x, y)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 4 — The Coordinate Plane](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)

### Critical point {#critical-point}

A point where $f'(x) = 0$ or $f'(x)$ does not exist — the only places a
smooth function can hide a local extremum.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/06-optimization/index.qmd)

### Cross entropy {#cross-entropy}

A measure $-\sum p_i \log q_i$ of how badly a predicted distribution $q$
matches a true distribution $p$; the standard classification loss in deep
learning.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

### Cross product {#cross-product}

A 3D vector built from two vectors, perpendicular to both, with magnitude
$|a||b|\sin\theta$ (= area of the parallelogram they span). Direction follows
the right-hand rule.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)

### Curl {#curl}

The local rotation of a vector field; $\nabla \times \mathbf{F}$ — a scalar
in 2D, a vector along the rotation axis in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

## D

### Decimal {#decimal}

A number written in base-10 place value, with columns continuing to the right
of a decimal point — each step right divides the value by ten.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Decimal point {#decimal-point}

The dot that marks the boundary between the ones column and the tenths column
in a decimal number. It is purely a position marker, not an operator.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Definite integral {#definite-integral}

The signed area between a function's graph and the x-axis over an interval
$[a, b]$, written $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. Defined as the limit of Riemann sums;
by the Fundamental Theorem, it equals $F(b) - F(a)$ for any antiderivative $F$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)

### Degree of a polynomial {#degree-of-a-polynomial}

The highest power of the variable that appears in a polynomial. The degree of
$3x^2 - 4x + 7$ is 2.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Derivative {#derivative}

The instantaneous rate of change of $f$ at $a$ — the limit
$\lim_{h \to 0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h$, equal to the slope of the tangent line.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Difference of squares {#difference-of-squares}

The pattern $x^2 - a^2 = (x + a)(x - a)$ — one of the most useful factoring
identities in algebra.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Differential {#differential}

The infinitesimal-language symbol $df = f'(x)\,dx$ — the linear approximation
written compactly.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Differential equation {#differential-equation}

An equation relating an unknown function to its own derivatives. Its
solutions are *functions*, not numbers; the most famous is $y' = ky$, whose
solution is exponential.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/12-differential-equations/index.qmd)

### Dimension {#dimension}

The size (number of vectors) of any basis of a vector space; the number of
independent degrees of freedom.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Discriminant {#discriminant}

The expression $b^2 - 4ac$ that appears under the square root in the
quadratic formula. Its sign tells you how many real roots the quadratic has.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

### Distributive {#distributive}

The rule that lets you split a multiplication across an addition:
$a \times (b + c) = a \times b + a \times c$. Geometrically, it's slicing a
rectangle into two smaller rectangles side by side.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Divergence {#divergence}

The scalar $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}$ measuring how much a vector field
spreads outward (positive: source) or contracts (negative: sink) at a point.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Divisibility {#divisibility}

A whole number $a$ is divisible by $b$ if $a \div b$ leaves no remainder —
equivalently, if $a$ is some whole-number multiple of $b$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Division {#division}

The inverse of multiplication: given a total and one factor, find the other
factor — equivalently, splitting a total into equal shares.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Dot product {#dot-product}

A scalar built from two vectors,
$a \cdot b = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + \cdots = |a||b|\cos\theta$.
Measures alignment: positive for acute angle, zero for perpendicular,
negative for obtuse.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)

## E

### Eigenvalue {#eigenvalue}

A scalar $\lambda$ such that $Av = \lambda v$ has a non-zero solution; a
root of $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. The "stretch factor" the matrix applies
along its preferred direction.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)

### Eigenvector {#eigenvector}

A non-zero vector that a matrix only stretches (does not rotate);
$Av = \lambda v$ for the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)

### Elimination method {#elimination-method}

A way to solve a system of equations: scale equations so that adding or
subtracting them cancels one variable, leaving a single-variable equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

### Ellipse {#ellipse}

A stretched-circle conic section — the set of points whose distances to two
fixed foci sum to a constant. Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/03-conic-sections/index.qmd)

### Entropy {#entropy}

The average number of bits needed to encode one outcome of a random
variable: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Maximised by uniform distributions,
zero for certainties.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

### Epsilon-delta definition {#epsilon-delta}

The rigorous definition of continuity at $a$: for every $\epsilon > 0$ there
exists $\delta > 0$ such that $|x - a| < \delta$ implies
$|f(x) - f(a)| < \epsilon$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Equation {#equation}

A statement that two expressions are equal. To solve an equation is to find
the value(s) of the variable that make it true.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### Equivalent fractions {#equivalent-fractions}

Two fractions naming the same number — obtained from one another by
multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same nonzero
number.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Euler's identity {#eulers-identity}

The equation $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$, joining the five constants $e, i, \pi, 1, 0$
in one formula. Often called the most beautiful equation in mathematics.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Expected value {#expected-value}

The long-run average of a random variable: $E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot P(X=x)$.
Generalises the arithmetic mean to weighted outcomes.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Exponent {#exponent}

The small raised number in an expression like $a^n$ that says how many copies
of the base are multiplied together.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)

## F

### Factorial {#factorial}

The product $n! = n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots 1$, with $0! = 1$ by convention. Counts
the number of orderings of $n$ distinct objects.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Factoring {#factoring}

Writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials. The reverse of
expanding a product. Example: $x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 12 — Factoring Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Fermat's Last Theorem {#fermats-last-theorem}

The statement that $a^n + b^n = c^n$ has no positive integer solutions for
any integer $n > 2$. Conjectured by Fermat in 1637 ("I have a truly
marvellous proof which this margin is too narrow to contain") and proven
by Andrew Wiles in 1995 via the Taniyama-Shimura conjecture on elliptic
curves and modular forms.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 13 — Elliptic Curves](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/13-elliptic-curves/index.qmd)

### Fermat's little theorem {#fermats-little-theorem}

If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 13 — Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/13-modular-arithmetic-rsa/index.qmd)

### Field {#field}

A commutative ring with identity in which every non-zero element has a
multiplicative inverse — so you can *divide*. Examples:
$\mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{F}_p$ for prime $p$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-rings-fields/index.qmd)

### Fourier series {#fourier-series}

The expression of a periodic function as an infinite sum of sines and
cosines, with coefficients computed by integration against the trig basis.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 12 — Fourier Series](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/12-fourier-series/index.qmd)

### Fourier transform {#fourier-transform}

An operation $\hat f(k) = \int f(x) e^{-ikx} dx$ that re-expresses a signal
as a function of frequency rather than time.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### Fraction {#fraction}

A number written $\tfrac{p}{q}$ — "p of q equal pieces of a whole".
Equivalently, the answer to the division $p \div q$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Function {#function}

A rule that assigns to each allowed input exactly one output. The same input
always returns the same output.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 1 — Functions as Machines](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/01-functions-as-machines/index.qmd)

## G

### Gaussian elimination {#gaussian-elimination}

The systematic algorithm of using three row operations to reduce a linear
system's augmented matrix to triangular form, then back-substituting.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 2 — Linear Systems and Gaussian Elimination](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)

### Geometric sequence {#geometric-sequence}

A sequence in which each term is the previous term times a fixed number,
called the common ratio. Example: $2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots$

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

### Gradient {#gradient}

The vector of all partial derivatives of a scalar-valued function; points in
the direction of steepest ascent, with magnitude equal to that maximum rate.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)

### Gradient descent {#gradient-descent}

The iterative rule $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\nabla L(w)$: take a small step
opposite the gradient to walk downhill on a loss landscape.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)

### Graph {#graph}

A pair $G = (V, E)$ of vertices and edges modelling pairwise relationships;
edges may be directed, weighted, or neither.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Graph Laplacian {#graph-laplacian}

The matrix $L = D - A$ (degree matrix minus adjacency matrix) of a graph;
symmetric, positive semi-definite, and its zero eigenvalues count connected
components.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/14-spectral-graph-theory/index.qmd)

### Greatest common divisor (GCD) {#gcd}

The largest whole number that divides both of two given whole numbers. Used
for simplifying fractions and in the Euclidean algorithm.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Group {#group}

A set with a single operation satisfying four axioms — closure,
associativity, identity, inverses — that abstracts every notion of symmetry,
from clock arithmetic to Rubik's cube.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/01-groups/index.qmd)

### Group action {#group-action}

A way for a group $G$ to "move" the points of a set $X$, with $e \cdot x = x$
and $(gh) \cdot x = g \cdot (h \cdot x)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/03-group-actions/index.qmd)

## H

### Handshake lemma {#handshake-lemma}

In any graph, the sum of vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges;
equivalently, the number of odd-degree vertices is even.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Heat equation {#heat-equation}

$u_t = \alpha\, u_{xx}$ (with multidimensional Laplacian in higher
dimensions). Models diffusion of heat or particles. Time-irreversible —
entropy increases.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Hilbert space {#hilbert-space}

A complete inner-product space — the infinite-dimensional analogue of
Euclidean space; the natural setting for Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics,
and modern PDE theory.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### Hyperbola {#hyperbola}

A two-branch conic section — the set of points whose distances to two foci
differ by a constant. Comet flybys trace hyperbolas.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/03-conic-sections/index.qmd)

### Hypotenuse {#hypotenuse}

The side of a right triangle opposite the right angle — always the longest
side.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 10 — The Pythagorean Theorem](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)

### Hypothesis test {#hypothesis-test}

A procedure that rejects a null hypothesis $H_0$ in favour of an alternative
$H_1$ when a test statistic falls in a region of low probability under $H_0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

## I

### Indeterminate form {#indeterminate-form}

A symbolic expression like $0/0$ or $\infty - \infty$ that does not by itself
fix the value of a limit; it signals more work is needed.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Initial condition {#initial-condition}

A specified value (and possibly derivative) at a single point that pins down
one solution out of the family of solutions to a differential equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/12-differential-equations/index.qmd)

### Integer {#integer}

A whole number — positive, negative, or zero. The set of integers is written
$\mathbb{Z}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Integration by parts {#integration-by-parts}

The product rule run backwards: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$. Useful when
one factor of the integrand becomes simpler under differentiation.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/10-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Intermediate Value Theorem {#intermediate-value-theorem}

A continuous function on $[a, b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ —
the basis of every bracketed root-finder.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Irrational number {#irrational-number}

A real number that cannot be written as a fraction of two integers — its
decimal expansion goes on forever without ever repeating. Examples:
$\sqrt{2}$, $\pi$, $e$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)

## J

*(No entries yet.)*

## K

*(No entries yet.)*

## L

### Lagrange's theorem {#lagranges-theorem}

In a finite group $G$, the size of every subgroup divides $|G|$ — and so
does the order of every element.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/01-groups/index.qmd)

### Laplace's equation {#laplaces-equation}

$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ (or $\nabla^2 u = 0$ in higher dimensions). Solutions
are *harmonic functions*; describe steady states of heat, electrostatic
potential, fluid flow potential.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Lattice {#lattice}

A discrete subgroup $L \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ of full rank — equivalently,
all integer combinations $\{\sum a_i b_i : a_i \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ of a fixed
basis $b_1, \ldots, b_n$. The same lattice has many bases (some "good",
near-orthogonal; some "bad", skewed), and that asymmetry is the foundation
of lattice-based post-quantum cryptography.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/14-lattice-crypto/index.qmd)

### Learning rate {#learning-rate}

The step-size parameter $\eta$ in a gradient-based optimiser: too small
wastes compute, too large diverges.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)

### Lebesgue integral {#lebesgue-integral}

An integral built by slicing the *output* axis of a function into level sets
and summing each level's measure times its value; agrees with the Riemann
integral on nice functions but handles wild ones and lets you swap limits
with integrals (Dominated Convergence, Monotone Convergence, Fatou's
lemma).

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/01-measure-theory/index.qmd)

### Limit {#limit}

The value $f(x)$ approaches as $x$ gets arbitrarily close to $a$, regardless
of whether $f(a)$ itself is defined.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Line {#line}

The path traced by moving in one fixed direction; a line segment is a finite
piece of a line with two endpoints.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Linear approximation {#linear-approximation}

$f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ — using the tangent line at $a$ as a
stand-in for $f$ near $a$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Linear equation {#linear-equation}

An equation in which the unknown appears only to the first power, with no
products or roots of unknowns. Its standard shape is $ax + b = c$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)

### Linear function {#linear-function}

A function of the form $y = mx + b$ whose graph is a straight line.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

### Linear inequality {#linear-inequality}

A statement that a linear expression is less than, greater than, or equal-or-
not-equal to a number. Its solution is a region of the number line, not a
single value.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 3 — Linear Inequalities](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/03-linear-inequalities/index.qmd)

### Logarithm {#logarithm}

The exponent you would have to raise a base to in order to reach a given
number. So $\log_{10}(1000) = 3$ because $10^3 = 1000$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 5 — Logarithms](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/05-logarithms/index.qmd)

## M

### Magnitude {#magnitude}

The length of a vector, computed by Pythagoras: $\|(x, y)\| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$
in 2D, $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

### Manifold hypothesis {#manifold-hypothesis}

The empirical claim that real high-dimensional data (images, text, audio)
lies near a much lower-dimensional curved surface inside its ambient space;
the basis for non-linear dimensionality reduction and modern generative
modelling.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 5 — Manifold Methods](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/05-manifold-methods/index.qmd)

### Mathematical induction {#mathematical-induction}

A proof technique establishing $P(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ by proving a base
case and that $P(n)$ implies $P(n+1)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Matrix {#matrix}

A rectangular array of numbers; viewed actively, an encoded linear
transformation whose columns are the images of the basis vectors.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 1 — Matrices and Matrix Operations](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd)

### Mean {#mean}

The "average" of a list of numbers: the sum divided by the count. The balance
point of the data.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Mean Value Theorem {#mean-value-theorem}

If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, there exists
$c \in (a,b)$ with $f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Measure {#measure}

A rule that assigns a non-negative size to subsets of a space such that the
size of a countable disjoint union equals the sum of the sizes
(σ-additivity); generalises length, area, volume, and probability into one
axiom.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/01-measure-theory/index.qmd)

### Median {#median}

The middle value of a sorted dataset (or the average of the two middle values
when the count is even).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Modular arithmetic {#modular-arithmetic}

Arithmetic done with remainders — the system that runs clocks, days of the
week, hash tables, and modern cryptography.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Mode {#mode}

The most frequently occurring value in a dataset. Useful for categorical
data.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Multiple integral {#multiple-integral}

A definite integral over a region in two or more dimensions, computed as
iterated single integrals (Fubini's theorem) with coordinates chosen to
match the region.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 6 — Multiple Integrals](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/06-multiple-integrals/index.qmd)

### Multiplication {#multiplication}

The total count of small squares in a rectangle of given width and height —
equivalently, repeated addition.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Multiplication principle {#multiplication-principle}

If a process has independent steps with $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ choices each,
the total number of outcomes is $n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_k$ — the foundation
of all counting.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Mutual information {#mutual-information}

The amount $I(X;Y)$ that knowing one random variable tells you about another;
zero exactly when they are independent.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

## N

### Natural exponential {#natural-exponential}

The function $e^x$, equal to its own derivative — base $e \approx 2.71828$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/05-derivatives-trig-exp-log/index.qmd)

### Natural logarithm {#natural-logarithm}

The inverse of $e^x$, written $\ln x$; its derivative is $1/x$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/05-derivatives-trig-exp-log/index.qmd)

### Natural number {#natural-number}

A counting number: $1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots$ — the answer to *"how many?"*. They
go on without end (no largest one) and are the foundation every other kind
of number is built from.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?](lessons/tier-1-foundations/01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)

### Negation {#negation}

The mirror of a number across zero. The negation of $5$ is $-5$; the negation
of $-5$ is $5$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Negative number {#negative-number}

A number to the left of zero on the number line — used wherever a "reference
zero" exists and you can be on either side of it.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Newton's method {#newtons-method}

The root-finding iteration $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$. Converges
quadratically near a simple root, given a good initial guess.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Normal distribution {#normal-distribution}

The bell-curve distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with PDF
$\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$; the universal
limiting distribution of sums of many independent random variables.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)

### Number {#number}

A quantity — the answer to *"how many?"* or *"how much?"*. The shapes we
write on paper (`3`, `12`, `1000`, `III`, `三`) are *names* for numbers,
not the numbers themselves; the same number can have many names across
cultures and notations.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?](lessons/tier-1-foundations/01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)

### Number line {#number-line}

A straight line on which every number has a single home — zero in the middle,
larger numbers to the right, smaller (and negative) numbers to the left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line](lessons/tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)

## O

### One-sided limit {#one-sided-limit}

The value $f(x)$ approaches as $x$ tends to $a$ from one side only — written
$\lim_{x \to a^+}$ (right) or $\lim_{x \to a^-}$ (left).

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Order of a numerical method {#order-of-a-numerical-method}

The exponent $p$ such that the per-step error scales as $O(h^p)$. Halving
$h$ multiplies accuracy by $2^p$. Euler $p=1$, trapezoidal $p=2$, RK4 /
Simpson $p=4$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Order of magnitude {#order-of-magnitude}

The exponent of 10 when a number is in scientific notation — how many tens
it would take to multiply up to that size.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/08-scientific-notation/index.qmd)

### Origin {#origin}

The point on the number line (or the coordinate plane) we choose to call
zero — the starting place from which every other number is measured.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line](lessons/tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)

## P

### PAC learning {#pac-learning}

"Probably Approximately Correct": a class is PAC learnable if some
algorithm, given polynomially many i.i.d. samples in $1/\epsilon$ and
$1/\delta$, returns a hypothesis whose error is at most $\epsilon$ with
confidence at least $1 - \delta$.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/04-statistical-learning-theory/index.qmd)

### Parabola {#parabola}

The conic section formed by slicing a cone parallel to one of its sides; the
U-shaped graph of any quadratic. Equidistant from a single focus and a
directrix line.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 2 — Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/02-parabola-graphing/index.qmd)

### Parametric curve {#parametric-curve}

A curve traced by a moving point $(x(t), y(t))$ as a parameter $t$ varies —
capturing both the *trail* of points and the *trip* of when each point is
visited.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/15-parametric-equations/index.qmd)

### Partial derivative {#partial-derivative}

The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one variable,
holding the others fixed; written $\partial f / \partial x_i$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)

### Partial differential equation {#partial-differential-equation}

An equation relating a function of several variables to its partial
derivatives. Examples: heat, wave, Laplace, Schrödinger.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### PDF (probability density function) {#pdf}

For a continuous random variable, the function whose area under the curve
between $a$ and $b$ gives the probability of falling in that interval. Not
a probability itself — a probability *density*.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)

### Perimeter {#perimeter}

The total distance around the boundary of a flat shape, measured in length
units (m, cm).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

### Permutation {#permutation}

An arrangement of $r$ items chosen from $n$ where order matters:
$P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Place value {#place-value}

The rule that the value of a digit depends on which column it sits in. In
our base-10 system, each step left multiplies the value by ten.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)

### PMF (probability mass function) {#pmf}

A function that assigns each value of a discrete random variable its
probability. The probabilities must sum to 1.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Point {#point}

A position with no size — the smallest object in geometry.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Polar form {#polar-form}

Writing a complex number as $z = re^{i\theta}$ where $r = |z|$ and
$\theta = \arg(z)$. Multiplication then becomes "scale by $r$, rotate by
$\theta$."

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Polygon {#polygon}

A closed flat shape with straight sides — triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon,
and so on.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Polynomial {#polynomial}

A sum of terms, each of the form (a number) × (a non-negative whole-number
power of $x$). Example: $3x^2 - 4x + 7$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Post-quantum cryptography {#post-quantum-cryptography}

Cryptographic schemes designed to remain secure against attackers equipped
with a large-scale quantum computer. RSA, ECC, and Diffie-Hellman are all
broken by Shor's algorithm; PQC candidates rely on problems (lattice
problems, code-based, hash-based, multivariate, isogeny) for which no
polynomial-time quantum algorithm is known. NIST standardised
CRYSTALS-Kyber (FIPS 203) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (FIPS 204) in 2024.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/14-lattice-crypto/index.qmd)

### Potential function {#potential-function}

A scalar function $f$ whose gradient equals a given vector field,
$\mathbf{F} = \nabla f$; such fields are called *conservative* and have zero
curl.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Power rule {#power-rule}

$\dfrac{d}{dx} x^n = n x^{n-1}$ for any real $n$ — the workhorse derivative
formula.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Prime {#prime}

A whole number $\ge 2$ with exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. The first
few are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Prime factorisation {#prime-factorisation}

The unique way of writing a whole number $\ge 2$ as a product of primes. The
Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic guarantees uniqueness.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Probability {#probability}

A number between 0 and 1 measuring how likely an event is. For equally likely
outcomes, $P(\text{event}) = \text{favourable outcomes} / \text{total outcomes}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd)

### Product rule {#product-rule}

$(fg)' = f'g + fg'$ — never $f'g'$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Proof {#proof}

A finite chain of justified steps deriving a conclusion from accepted
assumptions; the standard certificate of mathematical truth.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Proportion {#proportion}

A statement that two ratios are equal; cross-multiplication finds the missing
piece.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### p-value {#p-value}

The probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing data at
least as extreme as what was actually seen.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

## Q

### Quadratic equation {#quadratic-equation}

An equation of the form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, with $a \ne 0$. Has 2, 1, or 0
real solutions depending on the discriminant.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

### Quaternion {#quaternion}

A 4-real-number $a + bi + cj + dk$ obeying Hamilton's relations
$i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1$; the unit quaternions form $S^3$ and
double-cover the rotation group $SO(3)$, giving the singularity-free
representation of 3D rotation used in animation, robotics, and spacecraft
attitude control.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 8 — Quaternions and 3D Rotations](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/08-quaternions/index.qmd)

### Quotient {#quotient}

The result of a division — each fair share when the total is split.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Quotient rule {#quotient-rule}

$(f/g)' = (f'g - fg')/g^2$ — "low d-high minus high d-low, all over low
squared."

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

## R

### Range {#range}

The largest value in a dataset minus the smallest — a simple measure of
spread.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Rate {#rate}

A ratio of two quantities with different units, like km per litre or rupees
per kg.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### Ratio {#ratio}

A comparison of two quantities by division — the relationship that survives
when both quantities are scaled by the same factor.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### Reciprocal {#reciprocal}

The fraction obtained by flipping numerator and denominator. Dividing by a
fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Related rates {#related-rates}

A class of problems where two quantities are linked by an equation and you
find one rate of change from the other by differentiating with respect to
time.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Remainder {#remainder}

What's left over after a division when the total doesn't split exactly. The
defining equation: total = divisor × quotient + remainder.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Removable discontinuity {#removable-discontinuity}

A point where a function fails to be continuous only because of a single
missing or wrong value — patchable by redefining $f(a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Repeating decimal {#repeating-decimal}

A decimal whose digits cycle forever in a fixed pattern, written with a bar
over the repeating block (e.g. $0.\overline{3}$). Every fraction is either
terminating or repeating.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Residue theorem {#residue-theorem}

$\oint_C f(z)\, dz = 2\pi i \sum_k \mathrm{Res}(f; a_k)$, summed over
singularities inside $C$. Lets contour integrals (and many real integrals)
be computed from local data at singular points.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Riemann sum {#riemann-sum}

A finite sum $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$ approximating area under a curve by
rectangles; converges to the definite integral as $\Delta x \to 0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)

### Right angle {#right-angle}

A quarter-turn — exactly $90°$. The corner of a book, a window, or a square.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Ring {#ring}

A set with two operations (add and multiply): an abelian group under $+$,
an associative $\cdot$, tied together by distributivity. Generalises the
integers.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-rings-fields/index.qmd)

### RSA {#rsa}

A widely used encryption scheme whose security rests on the difficulty of
factoring a product of two large primes.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

## S

### Sample space {#sample-space}

The set of every possible outcome of a random experiment. Every probability
is computed relative to it.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd)

### Scientific notation {#scientific-notation}

A way of writing any number as $a \times 10^n$ with $1 \le a < 10$ and $n$
an integer — the shape and size kept separate.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/08-scientific-notation/index.qmd)

### Secant line {#secant-line}

A line passing through two points on a curve; its slope is the average rate
of change between them.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Second derivative test {#second-derivative-test}

At a critical point $c$: $f''(c) < 0$ → local max; $f''(c) > 0$ → local min;
$f''(c) = 0$ → inconclusive.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/06-optimization/index.qmd)

### Separation of variables {#separation-of-variables}

The technique of seeking PDE solutions of the product form
$u(x, t) = X(x)\,T(t)$, which (for linear PDEs on nice domains) reduces a
PDE to two ODEs joined by a shared separation constant.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Sequence {#sequence}

An ordered list of numbers, written $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$. Each position
has its own term.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

### Series {#series}

The running sum of a sequence — what you get by adding the first term, then
the first two, then the first three, and so on. A series can converge to a
finite number or diverge to infinity.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 4 — Sequences and Series](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/04-sequences-series/index.qmd)

### Set {#set}

A collection of distinct objects, with operations union, intersection,
difference, and complement; the foundational data type of modern mathematics.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Similar figures {#similar-figures}

Two figures that have the same shape but possibly different size —
corresponding angles equal, corresponding sides in the same ratio.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)

### Slope {#slope}

For a straight line, the change in $y$ per unit change in $x$ — its rise
over run. Captures how steeply the line climbs.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

### Smooth manifold {#smooth-manifold}

A topological space that locally looks like $\mathbb{R}^n$, with overlapping
coordinate charts whose transition maps are infinitely differentiable; the
natural setting for differential calculus on curved spaces.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/07-differential-geometry/index.qmd)

### Solution of an equation {#solution-of-an-equation}

A value of the unknown that, when substituted back in, makes both sides of
the equation equal.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)

### Spectral theorem {#spectral-theorem}

Every real symmetric matrix factors as $A = QDQ^T$ with $Q$ orthogonal and
$D$ diagonal of real eigenvalues; an orthonormal eigenbasis always exists.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### Square-cube law {#square-cube-law}

When you scale a figure by factor $k$, lengths multiply by $k$, areas by
$k^2$, and volumes by $k^3$. The reason a giant ant could not stand and an
elephant has thick legs.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)

### Square root {#square-root}

The non-negative number that, multiplied by itself, gives a chosen
non-negative number. The square root of 9 is 3 because $3 \times 3 = 9$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)

### Standard deviation {#standard-deviation}

The square root of variance, $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ — spread in
the original units of $X$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Standard error {#standard-error}

The standard deviation of a sampling distribution; for a sample mean it is
$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Substitution method {#substitution-method}

A way to solve a system of equations: isolate one variable in one equation,
then plug that expression into the other equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

### Subtraction {#subtraction}

Taking one number away from another — pictured on the number line as a hop
to the left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### SVD {#svd}

Singular Value Decomposition: every matrix factors as $A = U\Sigma V^T$ with
$U, V$ orthogonal and $\Sigma$ diagonal non-negative; the universal
generalisation of the spectral theorem.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### System of linear equations {#system-of-linear-equations}

Two or more linear equations considered together. A solution is a set of
values that satisfies every equation at once.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

## T

### Tangent line {#tangent-line}

The line that just touches a curve at a single point and matches its slope
there — the limit of secant lines.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Tangent space {#tangent-space}

At a point $p$ on a smooth manifold $M$, the vector space $T_pM$ of
velocities of curves through $p$; the linear approximation that lets you
do calculus and host the Riemannian metric.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/07-differential-geometry/index.qmd)

### Terminating decimal {#terminating-decimal}

A decimal that ends after finitely many digits — equivalently, a fraction
whose reduced denominator's only prime factors are 2 and 5.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Topology {#topology}

A choice of which subsets of a space count as "open," closed under arbitrary
unions and finite intersections; that one choice is enough to define
continuity, convergence, compactness, and connectedness without any
reference to distance.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/02-topology/index.qmd)

### Tree {#tree}

A connected graph with no cycles; on $n$ vertices it has exactly $n-1$ edges.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Triangle {#triangle}

A polygon with three straight sides; its three interior angles always sum to
$180°$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Trigonometric function {#trigonometric-function}

A function (sine, cosine, tangent, and their reciprocals) that converts an
angle into a number — built from the coordinates of a point travelling around
the unit circle.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 7 — Trig Ratios](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/07-trig-ratios/index.qmd)

## U

### Uncertainty principle {#uncertainty-principle}

The Fourier-pair inequality $\sigma_x \sigma_k \ge 1/2$: a signal cannot be
sharply localised in both time and frequency. In quantum mechanics this
becomes Heisenberg's $\sigma_x \sigma_p \ge \hbar/2$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### u-substitution {#u-substitution}

An integration trick that runs the chain rule backwards: rename an inner
expression as $u$, replace $dx$ with $du$, and the integral often simplifies
to a standard form.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/10-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Unit circle {#unit-circle}

The circle of radius 1 centred at the origin; the geometric foundation of
the trigonometric functions.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 8 — The Unit Circle](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)

### Unit vector {#unit-vector}

A vector of magnitude 1 — pure direction with the size stripped away. Found
by dividing any non-zero vector by its magnitude.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

## V

### Variable {#variable}

A letter that stands for a number — sometimes one specific unknown, sometimes
any number in a range.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### VC dimension {#vc-dimension}

The largest number of points a hypothesis class can label every possible
way (shatter); a finite VC dimension is exactly the condition for PAC
learnability and controls the generalisation gap as $\sqrt{d/n}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/04-statistical-learning-theory/index.qmd)

### Variance {#variance}

The expected squared deviation of a random variable from its mean:
$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$. Measures spread in squared units.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Vector {#vector}

A quantity with both magnitude and direction, written as a tuple of
coordinates such as $(x, y)$ in 2D or $(x, y, z)$ in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

### Vector field {#vector-field}

A function that assigns a vector to every point of a region — a "sea of
arrows" describing flow, force, or velocity.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Vector space {#vector-space}

A set on which addition and scalar multiplication are defined and satisfy
the standard eight axioms — the abstract setting that unifies arrows,
polynomials, and function spaces.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Velocity vector {#velocity-vector}

For a parametric curve $(x(t), y(t))$, the vector $(x'(t), y'(t))$ —
pointing in the direction of motion, with length equal to the speed.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/15-parametric-equations/index.qmd)

### Volume {#volume}

The size of the space inside a 3D shape, measured in cubic units (m³, cm³).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

## W

### Wave equation {#wave-equation}

$u_{tt} = c^2\, u_{xx}$. Models vibrating strings, sound, light. d'Alembert's
solution: $u(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ — left- and right-moving waves at
speed $c$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

## X

*(No entries yet.)*

## Y

### y-intercept {#y-intercept}

The value of $y$ where a graph crosses the y-axis — equivalently, the value
of $y$ when $x = 0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

## Z

### Zero {#zero}

The number meaning "nothing of this". As a placeholder it keeps other digits
in their proper columns; without it, "305" collapses into "35".

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)

### Zero-product property {#zero-product-property}

If a product of two numbers is zero, then at least one of them must be zero.
The hinge that turns a factored quadratic into its roots.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

---

::: {.applied-callout}
**For authors.** Every term that a lesson formally introduces in its `## Name`
beat must also have an entry here. See **CLAUDE.md → Glossary cross-reference
convention** for the exact authoring rule and the entry template.
:::
